speaker: interviewer
text: 17th, 1996. The interviewee is General Felix Sparks. Interviewer is Toni [INAUDIBLE] Binstock, in Lakewood, Colorado, United States of America. The interview is being conducted in English. General Sparks, you were just, at the end of the last tape, telling us about this-- that you had had a friend compile this for you. Finish explaining about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes this was a retired inspector general from the army. He was a long-time friend. Spent a lot of time, days and days, trying to research all the records pertaining to Dachau. And he did. This is probably the most complete document on existence relating to all peripheral things the days before, the days after what happened. These include army orders, corps orders, 42nd Division orders, 45th Division orders, all battalions journals. Everything that could possibly relate to Dachau, he dug up. But the important thing that he dug up was the inspector general's report of the investigation and the subsequent action by the commanding general of the 7th United States Army and the 3rd United States Army. Well, this came as an eye-opener to me. Because one reason, the reason it couldn't be found, most of the documents have been classified as secret and were not available until declassification took place of-- slowly over the years, the various documents have been declassified. So it was after the declassification that took place that this colonel was able to find all of these records. And he has in here the complete report of all the testimony of all the witnesses in the case, which is over 100 pages long. There's several pages in this, in this thing which he sent me. But he has a complete report, the action of the higher headquarters. And as I say, it came as an eye-opener to me. Because I was never informed of any charges against anybody except General Patton said there were some serious charges. But he didn't say what. What I found out from the inspector general's report was that he had recommended that several people be tried for the murder of prisoners, some of my men, and then much to my surprise, also some men from the 42nd Division, which puzzled me. But after I got to reading all the testimony and all the pertinents documents, then long unanswered questions came to light. Actually, the 42nd Division was involved more than I thought originally. What happened was that we had been in there about an hour or so. And according to the testimony of General Linden, the principal witness, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he said that he came driving up to the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dachau, this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dachau. And he heard some, he heard some firing inside of the, of the compound. He then ordered his-- couple of his officers to go in and see what was happening. Well, of course, that was my men. And there was sporadic firing going on the whole time. In addition to killing the-- some German guards, the men also dispatched a number of large rather vicious guard dogs that were in there. I've never heard that mentioned before by the 42nd or anybody else, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the-- whose guard dogs were these?

speaker: interviewee
text: They used them to patrol the exterior of, of the prison compound.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The German dog, oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There were a miscellaneous assortment of dogs. Most of them I saw were Dobermans. But my men shot them. Anyway, I discovered that-- what-- as that came as a surprise to me that prior to my run-in with General Linden and a, a small party, the 42nd had been in there before that. What happened-- another surprise to me came that nobody ever mentioned the presence of this Marguerite Higgins. Nobody ever mentioned her presence. In all the testimony, the 42nd never mentioned her presence, although they made one slip-up that I found. In their division journal, I found that she had reported to their headquarters that morning. That's the only reference to her. And that was, I say, a slip-up, I think, on their part. They didn't recall recording that. But it's in here. Anyway, they never mentioned-- any of the men from the 42nd division never mentioned that she was with General Linden. And he never mentioned it at all. She mentioned it, of course, in a press dispatches. And it's been recorded as a book I have here, in which she states that she was in there with the 42nd. What I discovered was that General Linden came up there with a small party, maybe 12, 14 men, something like that, mostly officers, and jeep drivers, and a few other enlisted, and I think an interpreter. And he sent a couple of his officers. And they went in. And they didn't say anything. Because my men were quite a distance away. Well, they heard the firing. And they came back said, well, we don't see anything. Well, they came in the main gate to the, the whole area, which is only two blocks from the prison compound. So they-- there was, according to General Linden, there was a couple of people who met him at the gate. He said one was a Red Cross representative. I've never figured that out. I don't think the Germans allowed any Red Cross people in the concentration camps. But I suspect it was a Gestapo man who put on a Red Cross band and said he was Red Cross. Because I'm certain the Germans did not allow the Red Cross into those concentration camps. Any event, there was another officer there. And they said that they surrendered. They told General Linden. So at that point, this party drove on in and right up to the gate of the confinement area, which my men had not yet reached. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So they drove up there. And the German guards surrendered to them. maybe 12 or 15 were at that [? shorthouse, ?] the entrance to the camp. And they came out with their hands up.

speaker: interviewee
text: The guards in a tower about 100 yards away, the five of them, came down and surrendered also. And at that point, they killed them. That was the five bodies that I saw, which over the years, I thought my men had killed. Well, at that point, the 42nd group panicked, in my opinion. Because they saw my men coming along the edge of the path there of the prison area. One platoon was coming along there. And they panicked. Because my men witnessed the killing of those five guys, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which I assumed my men had done all these years. They immediately took off and went on to the main gate. It was only two blocks away. And they had prisoners with them, probably 12, 15. And they stayed there then for a little while. And I didn't even know they had been there. And when I came along, there was no 42nd Division people there, just a lieutenant, a Lieutenant Strickland that was one of my platoon leaders. And that's when the prisoners erupted and the subsequent events. So I would say it was about 15 minutes later that General Linden came back in with the, with the three, three jeeps and had Marguerite Higgins in the second jeep. She described it, although, in her words, she was the first one in there. In her book, which was a biography of her, she writes that a German SS general met her at the main gate and escorted-- sent a lieutenant to escort her through the camp. Hell, there wasn't an SS general within 100 miles of Dachau. The commandant was a colonel, but he had long since gone before we ever got there. But that was her story anyway. But she admits-- she said that she had some words with an officer from the 45 Division, which was me. And she claims that she-- I grabbed her by the shoulder. Well, I didn't touch the woman. I did order her out, but I never laid a hand on her. That's her story anyway. But the strange part of it is, it was never mentioned. In the lengthy investigation, the 42nd never mentioned her presence. And-- but they were there. And. They took some prisoners. But they did not-- were not the first there. And they were not the ones who liberated the camp. That's what they claim now.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this now is record that--

speaker: interviewee
text: This record-- they admit they weren't there till 3:30 that afternoon in the sworn testimony. And they admit-- well, nobody admits killing those guards. They just-- one of the sergeants said, well, I-- they're all standing there. And somebody starts shooting them. And they did. They killed all of them. And at that point, they decided they better get out of there. Because my men were right on the scene. My men saw the shooting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever talk to your men about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't know about it. I never had an opportunity, you see, because I was gone a couple days later. And I-- you know, a lot of my men are still alive. They were there. They know what happened. But I've never been able to find Lieutenant Strickland. But the company commander, Lieutenant Walsh, is very much alive. He lives in Pennsylvania. He was a commander who was there and saw the whole thing, along with a large number of his men and some of my men as well. But the strange thing about the investigation, some of the witnesses testified that I was the one who stopped the shooting with a machine gun. So no charges were filed against me, apparently for that reason. But about four or five of my men were-- five, I think, about three or four from the 42nd Division that participated in the killing of those five guards were charged with murder, which is a very serious offense, of course a general court martial. Well, it was, I say, illuminating to me. Because now I have been able to put together all of the pieces and know exactly what happened. The 42nd Division says I make up all these things. I made up all this stuff. Well, I was there. I didn't make up anything. I didn't have to make up anything. So that's the way it is today. But they're still claiming that, and they're writing nasty letters to the Department of History and so on. And I don't give a damn myself who liberated it. I'm sorry that I was ever there. The only comfort we got out of that whole incident was that we were able to free several thousand poor souls who would-- were dying daily before our arrival. So many of them, at least, were able to resume some out of a halfway normal life. I have met several of them who were there. Some live here in Denver. I've spoken around the country on this subject. And I've met quite a few. So that was the only comfort we got out of the whole thing. And who was there first or second makes no difference to me. I just don't like him attacking me personally or my company commander or me by people who weren't there. All these people doing the attacks are people who were not there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do these, these survivors that you have met, who do they say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they don't know. They were in such shape, they were in such bad shape. We didn't go around saying we're from this company or this division, you see? They were in such bad shape that they just knew that American troops were there. They say there was no way that we could identify. Well, we didn't try to identify ourselves-- no point in it. It was such pandemonium and such a strain there that we never even thought about any long-term aftermath about who was there first or who was there second. Really doesn't make any difference. The 42nd had some men their. they had about 12, 14. And they got to the prison compound before we did. But we'd been there over an hour before they got there, probably more like two hours before they got there. So-- and they really didn't have anything to do with the liberation as such. Because I had the one that had the troops there. They did not. Their 12, 14 men did nothing except to kill-- they took some prisoners. And they killed those five. Well, a subsequent investigation turned out there were something like 30, 34 dead German soldiers. Books have been written that we killed 500 and something. Actually, it was probably not more than 250 in the total camp when we got there. Because most of them had already left. And they just left the low-ranking enlisted men. And I saw a couple of lieutenants. The camp commandant and all the high-ranking officers, the SS people, had fled there before we arrived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's really immaterial at this point. I just don't like the nasty letters. And I got from anonymous letters and-- telling me I'm a liar. I got a whole stack of stuff that General Linden's son has written to me, calling me a liar, that I made up all this thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that gets my dander up a little bit. I'll, I'll go before any forum in the world with any number of people to explain what happened if some forum wants to decide. I've got a lot of men still alive who were there. They're not all liars. And they're, you know, people who have done pretty well in life. The C Company commander is still alive that when it relieve me. The L Company commander is dead. But some of the enlisted men are-- my interpreter, still very much alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they never-- the, the government never called those people or you as witness to all of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: They called them, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: They did call them.

speaker: interviewee
text: My men were-- some of my party, my interpreter and my runner, were witnesses in the investigation. But they never called me, said I was not available. I wasn't for about a week when I came back. But I was only there about 10 days. But they knew damn well I was back. Nobody wanted to question me. That's the problem. I was a key witness in that thing. Nobody wanted to hear my story. Because I was bucking a general who was a West Point graduate, I might add. I'm not a West Point graduate. One peculiar thing also that happened, there was another-- the two officers in a jeep who were following General Linden were both West Point officers, Colonel [? Bolduc, ?] who was the regimental commander, and Colonel Valens, who was a battalion commander. Now, Valens testified at great length. But Colonel [? Bolduc ?] was never called as a witness, either. And he was there all the time. He became the chief of staff for the 42nd Division. He was never questioned. And he was one of the key witnesses. My guess is that Colonel [? Bolduc ?] wouldn't back up what the others have said. I think he was an honorable man and would not back up the stories told by Valens and Lindon. Lindon, in fact, says he never saw-- there was some shooting. But he never saw those German soldiers being killed. The hell he didn't. He was right there on the spot. And Valens was right there on the spot. And Valens said, m he shot at them. But his gun jammed. But in his previous story, he made a great mistake. He wrote a long account of that right after that, in which he said he and his men charged this guard tower with blazing rifles and killed everybody in there. That's on the record. That's in the investigation also. But that-- when he came to testify on oath, he changed the story completely. Said, well, yeah. But his gun jammed. And he said he was carrying a 45 submachine-- caliber submachine gun. What the hell a battalion commander is doing carrying a 45 subcaliber machine gun, I do not know. That's not the normal-- normal armament for field-grade officers it is a 45 caliber pistol. But he admits that he was carrying a 45 Tommy-- what we call a Tommy gun, but that it jammed and he didn't fire. Like he said in his first article, he was trying to make himself out a great hero to his-- I guess his division commander. And he wrote this long narrative before the investigation. [LAUGHS] And he got caught on it when they came along for the sworn testimony. But none of them mentioned that Marguerite Higgins was there. And that was the only reason, in my opinion, that they were there. They brought her from the division headquarters. The division record shows that she reported into division headquarters. Doesn't say what happened after that. But I'm certain that the only reason that Linden and that small party came there was to permit her to interview the prisoners, but not a word about that. It didn't look good for a commanding general to go out-- or assistant division commander to go out of his area with a nice-looking young babe in somebody else's territory so that she could conduct interviews. But that's the sole reason they were there. There was no other reason for him to be there whatsoever. Wasn't in his combat zone at all. And Corps backed that up. Corps said-- there's a message of corps says that is 42nd-- 45th division area. That's in the record. When our commanding general complained about 42nd Division being put in there, the message from corps came back, said that is your responsibility. That's in your zone of action, not the 42nd Division. That's on record. And it isn't in that book.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are you going to do with this book, General?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Holocaust Museum in Washington, DC, heard about the records that I have. And they want me to mail this to them. And then they will copy every bit of it and return it to me. And then I'm going to donate it to the 45th Infantry Division Museum, which is a beautiful museum in Oklahoma City about the 45th Infantry Division, probably the finest division museum in the United States. It's the one maintained by the 45th Infantry Division in Oklahoma City. The state government of Oklahoma strongly supports that museum. And it has a full-time staff and tremendous exhibits. I'm going to give that book to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you still get together with some of your wartime buddies from World War II.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, every year. We'll be meeting 1st of October in Orlando, Florida. And some of the principals at Dachau will be there-- the company commander, Lieutenant Walsh, I Company, Lieutenant Edmonds, the C Company commander, oh, and a number of other people, enlisted people, who were present at the time also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe they'll want to give their testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we could all give some, all right. My runner, my rifleman, is dead now.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his--

speaker: interviewee
text: Colonel Johnson. But my interpreter is still alive. My executive officer, Major Kessler, has, has died. Not many people left. But there are probably 25 or 30 soldiers that are still alive who were in there on the first day-- at least 25 or 30. Some of them I have-- well, probably more. But a lot of them-- it's difficult to locate people after passage of 50 years. I've never been able to find Lieutenant Strickland. But we've searched records and searched. But we're all now getting up close to 80 or in our 80s. So there are not, not too many World War II veterans left. If you served in World War II, you got to be at least 75 today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let's go on with your, your career. After you came home in August of '45, then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had just gotten-- been back to the States a few days when suddenly, the war with Japan was over. The atomic bomb had been dropped. And shortly after that, Japan surrendered, which was around the 1st or 2nd of December in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- December?

speaker: interviewee
text: September-- right around the 1st of September in '45. I had just been back to the States three or four days when that happened. And the 45th Division was almost immediately-- all of the-- most of the National Guard and Reserve Division-- well, all of the National Guard and Reserve divisions were then demobilized. They were removed from federal service. They were shipped back to the States. And everybody that wanted to discharge was discharged. And the units were terminated. They did-- no, no longer existed. They were-- some of them reformed after that, back in their original status. But I, I wanted to-- I had been in law school at the University of Arizona when I got my notice to report to the Army, which was in December of 1940. So I was anxious to get back to law school. I was anxious to see my two and 1/2 year old son that I had never seen. So I immediately applied for termination. And it was granted. I then started back to law school and went summers and all and completed it at the University of Colorado. I got my degree in 1947, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] moved to Western Colorado. On the following election, I was elected district attorney over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Delta, Colorado. That's in Western Colorado. I have seven counties in my district. And I had a private practice of law, had a law firm. I had formed a law firm called Sparks, Conklin, and [? Carol-- ?] three of us. And we-- in the private practice, and besides, I was a district attorney. At that time, district attorneys could also have a private practice. That's not the case anymore in Colorado. Now district attorneys are full-time. Well, I practiced there for 10 years or so and then was appointed to the Colorado Supreme Court as a justice in the Colorado Supreme Court, served out an unexpired term of one of the justices who had died. And then I had various-- I became a special assistant attorney general with the state of Colorado and was sort of a lobbyist for the state of Colorado and Washington for about 20 years and retired about in 1979.

speaker: interviewer
text: You retired from--

speaker: interviewee
text: I retired-- I got-- after I got out of the service, I was in the Reserves. And then I decided-- I had never been in the National Guard, but I had served with the National Guard regiment in the whole war. So I joined the National Guard here in Colorado. I ended up as a commanding general of the Colorado Army National Guard and retired from that in 1977. In 1979, I retired from everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let me go back a little. You, you came back to the US in '45 and reunited with your wife and child. Were there other children born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, later, yeah, there were. About 10 months after I got back, our oldest daughter was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jean Ann. She lives here in Lakewood now, got three children of her own. And then two or three years later, a son was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Scott. And then a couple of years later, my last child was born, a daughter, Kim. All of them are here in the metropolitan area. We now have six grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe we'll stop the tape here and continue on the next one.
